coredata.dc:title: A ternary polymer nanocomposite film composed of green-synthesized graphene quantum dots, polyaniline, polyvinyl butyral and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate for supercapacitor application
coredata.dc:creator: Arthisree, D.
Madhuri, W.
Saravanan, N.
Dinesh, B.
Saikrithika, S.
Kumar, Annamalai Senthil
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their enhanced electrical conductivity, flexibility, cost-effective and simple sampling methods, polymer-carbon nanocomposite material composed of conducting polymers have been referred as an elegant system for supercapacitor applications. Indeed, integration of engineering polymer, which has an insulating property, such as Polyvinyl butyral (PVB), into the conducting matrix is a challenging task. Herein, we introduce a ternary polymer nanocomposite composed of PVB, PANI, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) and green-synthesized GQD based functionally stable and optically active system for an efficient supercapacitor application. The polymer-carbon nanocomposite has been prepared as a film by the solution-casting method. The “as prepared” polymeric film was characterized using XRD, TEM, Raman, IR, UV-Vis spectroscopic, laser-optical profilometric imaging and electrochemical techniques. From the collective results, it has been revealed that there is a chemical interaction between the GQD and polymeric systems, presumably due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Supercapacitor application of the polymer nanocomposite was demonstrated by simply modifying the composite mixture on a conducting substrate-disposable screen-printed carbon electrode. Cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance and galvanostatic charge/discharge experiments were performed with the modified electrode. 1 wt. % GQD loaded ternary polymer-nanocomposite is found to be an optimal system for supercapacitor application. This material showed a capacitance value of 4998 Fg−1cm−2
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ternary polymer nanocomposite
Polyvinyl butyral
Polyaniline
PEDOT:PSS, Green synthesized graphene quantum dots
Dimensionally stable supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD} composites and its modified screen-printed carbon electrodes
Physicochemical characterization of the hybrid polymer nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of various polymer nanocomposite systems dissolved in DMF solvent; a. (i-v) PVB, PANI, PEDOT:PSS, PVB+PAN+PEDOT:PSS, GQD, b (i-iii) PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% loaded nanocomposites

X-ray diffraction pattern of various polymer nanocomposite systems; a. i. PVB, ii. PANI, b.(i-iv) PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% loaded nanocomposites
TEM images of a. GQD, b. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend and c. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% nanocomposite
FTIR spectrum of a. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend, b. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5wt.%, c. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1wt.% and d. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% loaded nanocomposite
Raman spectra of a. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend, b. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5wt.%, c. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1wt.% and d. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% loaded nanocomposite
Optical scanning profile images of A. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend and B. PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt. % nanocomposite at 1mm × 1mm scale.

a&b (i-iv). CV responses of PANI, PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS blend, PEDOT:PSS, GQD, c. (i-iii) CV response of PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% in 0.5 M KCl and 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3− solution at v=50mVs−1

Electrochemical impedance spectra of various polymer nanocomposite systems a. (i-iv) PANI, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS blend, PEDOT:PSS, GQD, b. (i-iii). PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+ GQD 0.5wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% in 0.5 M KCl and 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3− solution

a&b. (i-iv) CV response of PANI, PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS blend, PEDOT: PSS, GQD, c. (i-iii) CV response of PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+ GQD 0.5 wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5wt.% in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution at v=10 mVs−1.

a (i-iv). Galvanostatic charge/discharge response of PANI, PEDOT: PSS, GQD, PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS blend, b. (i-iii) Galvanostatic charge/discharge response PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 0.5 wt.%, PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% and PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1.5 wt.% at an applied current bias value, 100μA in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution
a. Scan rate on the CV of PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% in 0.1 M H2SO4. b. Charge-discharge cycles of PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% carried out at different applied current bias values.
The continuous charge-discharge cycles of PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% nanocomposite in the potential range of 0–1 V at different applied bias currents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PANI-Polyaniline; PEDOT:PSS- poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate; GQD- Graphene quantum dots; PVB-polyvinyl butyral. Condition: 5 mM Fe(CN)63- in 0.5M KCl and 5mM of [Fe(CN)6]3− at an applied potential = 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl.
PANI-Polyaniline; PEDOT:PSS- poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate; GQD- Graphene quantum dots; PVB-polyvinyl butyral

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Literature comparison of capacitance values of various multi polymer composite systems.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MWCNT-Multiwall carbon nanotube; PHQ-poly(hydroquinone);PE-Phenol formaldehyde; Zn-Zinc; PVB-Polyvinyl butyral; PVP-Polyvinylpyrrolidone; PAN-Polyacrylonitrile; PMMA-Polymethyl methacrylate; CNF-Carbon nanofiber; PPy-Polypyrrole; PANI-Polyaniline; PEDOT:PSS- poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate; GQD- Graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: A ternary polymer nanocomposite film composed of green-synthesized graphene quantum dots, polyaniline, polyvinyl butyral and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate for supercapacitor application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ceramic Composite Laboratory, Centre for Functional Materials, Department of Physics, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCeramic Composite Laboratory, Centre for Functional Materials, Department of Physics, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano and Bioelectrochemical Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNano and Bioelectrochemical Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CO2 Research and Green Technology Centre, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CO2 Research and Green Technology Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCO2 Research and Green Technology Centre, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their enhanced electrical conductivity, flexibility, cost-effective and simple sampling methods, polymer-carbon nanocomposite material composed of conducting polymers have been referred as an elegant system for supercapacitor applications. Indeed, integration of engineering polymer, which has an insulating property, such as Polyvinyl butyral (PVB), into the conducting matrix is a challenging task. Herein, we introduce a ternary polymer nanocomposite composed of PVB, PANI, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) and green-synthesized GQD based functionally stable and optically active system for an efficient supercapacitor application. The polymer-carbon nanocomposite has been prepared as a film by the solution-casting method. The “as prepared” polymeric film was characterized using XRD, TEM, Raman, IR, UV-Vis spectroscopic, laser-optical profilometric imaging and electrochemical techniques. From the collective results, it has been revealed that there is a chemical interaction between the GQD and polymeric systems, presumably due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Supercapacitor application of the polymer nanocomposite was demonstrated by simply modifying the composite mixture on a conducting substrate-disposable screen-printed carbon electrode. Cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance and galvanostatic charge/discharge experiments were performed with the modified electrode. 1 wt. % GQD loaded ternary polymer-nanocomposite is found to be an optimal system for supercapacitor application. This material showed a capacitance value of 4998 Fg−1cm−2

ce:keyword.ce:text: PEDOT:PSS, Green synthesized graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times there is a great attention in the field of electrical energy storage owing to the enormous demand for power-efficient portable electronic devices [1]. Supercapacitor is one of the capable energy storage systems which lie in between the batteries and capacitor systems [2,3]. In this regard, various carbons [4,5], metal oxides [6], metal-organic frameworks [7,8], Prussian blue analogs [9] and conducting polymers [10] have been used. In consideration of low cost, easy preparation and high supercapacitor activity, carbon-based materials have been referred as a promising system [11]. In the above cases, the capacitance values in rage of 1000-2000 mF cm−2 have been achieved [4,6,7,10,11]. There are significant efforts made to improve the capacitance value by combining the carbon nanomaterials with various conducting polymers as a hybrid material system [12-14]. Conducting polymer such as polyaniline (PANI) is considered as a vital electrode material for the electrical and supercapacitor activities, and the achieved capacitance values are in the range of 500-1000 Fg−1 [15]. Indeed, relatively low electrical conductivity and stability limiting them for the extension to real practical systems [15,16]. Meanwhile, the design and development of new electrode materials, which could show enhanced electrical and supercapacitor property is continued research in the interdisciplinary areas of material science, polymer chemistry and energy storage systems. Herein, we report on graphene quantum dots (GQD) based ternary polymer nanocomposite that has been prepared by combining green synthesized GQD from biomass, PANI, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) and polyvinyl butyral (PVB) as a hybrid polymer film system for stable and efficient supercapacitor applications.

Polymers like PANI, PEDOT: PSS and Polypyrrole (PPy) (set-I) [17] that show excellent electrical properties are not capable enough to form stable thin film-like structures. On the other hand, polymers like PVB, polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (set-II) [18] which show good mechanical film property are not suitable for electrical applications. Thus, by properly combing the above two sets of polymers at an appropriate ratio, one can achieve properties of the polymeric film and electrical conductivity for technological applications. Since, PANI has been referred as an efficient conducting polymer suitable for its potential in supercapacitance activity [19], in this work, PANI along with another πe-s conjugated polymer, PEDOT: PSS has been chosen to obtain an improved activity. The conjugated π e-s in the PEDOT: PSS has facilitated a π-π transition with PANI for an enhanced electrical and supercapacitor property [20]. Keeping this in mind, on trial and error basics, we developed a new hybrid polymer nanocomposite system that has been integrated with set-I and set-II polymeric networks, along with the minimum quantity of GQD loading.

GQD is a revolutionary carbon material that has been widely used for optical, electrical and electrochemical energy systems. In general, these materials are synthesized by hydrothermal methodology utilizing a variety of precursor chemicals like glucose [21], citric acid [22], cornflour [23], rice husk [24] etc. Due to the meager molecular dimension, GQD shows efficient optical and fluorescence activity, and hence several optoelectronic materials have been developed by utilizing it. Indeed, the energy-related application has been rarely reported with this material [25-27]. High water solubility and difficulty in preparing a stable GQD modified electrode are the likely reasons for the failure. A new methodology adopted in this work to develop GQD based ternary polymer composite electrode has shown highly stable and efficient electrochemical activity of the carbon composite material. It has been found that the effect of GQD loading on the polymer composite plays a crucial role in achieving an optimal electrical and supercapacitor activity of the composite system. A high supercapacitor value, 4998 Fg−1cm−2, was achieved with this new hybrid material in this work. It is about 2-10 times higher value than that of recently reported GQD based polymer nanocomposite systems (polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) /PEDOT/GQD, PPy/sulfur-doped GQD, PPy/GQD) [25-27]. This report covers a systematic preparation, characterization and supercapacitor application of GQD-ternary polymer composite.

Polyvinyl butyral powder, molecular weight >72,000, was obtained from Kurary, India Pvt., New Delhi. Polyaniline (emeraldine salt) powder with an average molecular weight >15,000, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrene sulfonate) l.3 wt. % aqueous dispersion in water, dimethylformamide (DMF), concentrated sulfuric acid, potassium chloride and ferricyanide− were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. GQD was prepared by the hydrothermal autoclave method using rice powder similar to the procedure adapted from our previous published paper [28]. Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) were obtained from Zensor R&D, Taiwan, as a gift sample. All the chemicals are used in analytical grade as received. Unless otherwise stated, all the voltammetric experiments were performed in 0.1 M H2SO4 system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD} composites and its modified screen-printed carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: At first, polymer and GQD stock solutions were prepared by the following procedure: 2.5 g PVB dissolved in 37.5 mL, 0.5 g PANI in 5 mL, 1 g PEDOT: PSS in 10 mL and 1 mL GQD in 6 mL of DMF solvent uniformly. Furthermore, four different concentrations of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD} were prepared by mixing at different weight fraction ratios, {5:2:3:0}; {5:2:2.5:0.5}; {5:2:2:1} & {5:2:1.5:1.5}. Before the film casting, the above dilute solutions of the polymers/GQD were mixed thoroughly by magnetically stirring for 12 hours continuously at room temperature, T=25±2°C. This mixture forms a colloidal solution that maintains stability for 6-8 hours. For polymer nanocomposite film formation, 10 ml of the “as prepared” solutions were drop-casted uniformly onto a clean glass petri-dish (10 cm diameter) and dried at room temperature for more 72±0.5 hours. According to the weight ratios, the “as-prepared” polymer hybrid composites were named as {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 0.5 wt.%}, {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} and {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1.5 wt. %} which has been compared with control samples of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS} and pure GQD systems. For electrochemical studies, 5 μl of the respective polymer composite solutions were drop-casted on a 3 mm diameter SPCEs and air-dried at room temperature for 2 hours. Scheme 1 provides a basic illustration for the structure of the hybrid polymer nanocomposite system.

X-ray diffraction pattern was recorded using Bruker AXS D8 focus advanced X‐ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan, Tokyo) instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral measurement was carried out using Shimadzu, IR affinity‐1 spectrometer. Raman spectroscopic technique was carried out using RENISHAW I via laser Raman microscope (He‒Ne laser λ=633 nm). A scanning optical profilometer (working area: 1mm × 1mm) studies were carried out using Versascan, Princeton Applied Research instrument, USA. UV‐Vis absorption spectrum experiment was obtained using Shimadzu UV‐2401PC spectrometer. Techni G2–20, TEM instrument was utilized to examine the particle dispersion and microstructures of the prepared nanocomposite material. Electrochemical experiments were performed using a CHI model 440E electrochemical workstation (USA) with a three-electrode system consisting of screen-printed carbon electrode (3 mm diameter size) chemically modified active polymer composite material as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference and platinum wire as the auxiliary electrodes with 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as an electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were done at scan rates of 10 and 50 mV s−1, and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) analysis was measured at a current density value of 0.14 Ag−1. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of various polymer nanocomposite systems was performed using 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3− dissolved in 0.5 M KCl solution at a biased potential 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the hybrid polymer nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To study the electronic transition mechanism of the prepared system, UV-Vis spectroscopy was performed. The UV-Vis spectra of PVB, PANI, PEDOT:PSS, GQD and {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} ternary polymer blend are depicted in Fig. 1 a(i-v). The absorbance of PVB showed a feeble peak at λmax=225 nm corresponding to the ketonic carbonyl group's electronic transition of PVB [29] and PANI that exhibit a broad peak at λ=272±2 nm owing to the π-π* transition of conjugation length of PANI (emeraldine salt) [30]. It is noted that PEDOT:PSS exhibits strong absorbance at λmax=277±2 nm due to π-n transition corresponding to the aromatic structure of PSS [31] and GQD has shown an intense peak at 284±2 nm due to n-π* transition of the surface C=O bonding [32]. Note that the ternary polymer blend {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} shows an intense peak at λmax=284±2 nm as a result of the combinatory peak assignment of all the pure systems (PVB, PANI, PEDOT:PSS, GQD, Fig. 1, curve i-iv). These observations indicate heterogeneous mixture like behaviour of the new polymer composite material, wherein, the individual components show their activity independently. Loading the polymer matrix with different wt.% of GQD (0.5-1.5wt.%) noticeably influenced the absorbance trend of the composite system, as shown in Fig. 1b (i-iii). As the loading wt.% of GQD in the composite increase, the absorbance at λ=282-280 nm tends to decrease considerably for the 1 wt. % loading composite {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%}, which may be due to the π-stacking like arrangement of GQD on PANI in the polymer matrix [33].

To understand the crystallinity nature of the composite material, the samples were subjected to XRD measurements. For this present study, XRD of the composite material was compared with individual polymers as a control experiment. Fig. 2 a graph i and ii are typical XRD patterns of pure polymers PVB and PANI. The pure PVB exhibits three broad and less intense peaks at 2θ=18ο, 30ο& 41ο, whereas the PANI shows a feeble peak at 2θ=25.53ο representing the amorphous nature of polymer [34]. The PANI-XRD peaks were matching with the JCPDS card number, 53-1890 for the crystalline polyaniline material. XRD patterns of the ternary polymer blend {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} and different GQD loaded nanocomposites, {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5-1.5 wt.%} were displayed in Fig. 2b (i-iv). The broad amorphous diffraction patterns were retained for all samples at a peak position around 2θ=42±2ο attributing heterogeneous nature of the polymer nanocomposite system. It is clearly seen that there is no new 2θ peak appeared in the composites supporting the observation.

To further understand the GQD-particle size and its distribution detail in the polymer matrix, the pure ternary polymer blend and 1 wt.% GQD loaded composites films were subjected to TEM analysis. Fig. 3 a depicts the TEM image of pure GQD with particle distribution in the range of 50±5 nm. Meanwhile, Fig. 3(b-c) shows the TEM images of polymer blend {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} and {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} composite system, in which some particles of polymer agglomeration were clearly seen. The enlarged view (Fig. 3c) of 1 wt.% GQD loading in the polymer system attributed a uniform dispersion of tiny dots like structured and well adsorbed GQD particles with a size around 6±2 nm in the polymer matrix. The observed particle size on the GQD in the composite is relatively lesser than that of the pure GQD, which is similar to a filler-like molecular arrangement in the polymer composite system. Partial interaction/immobilization of monolayers of GQD with the polymer surface in the composite system [33] is the likely reason for such observation. Further, the functional group interaction between polymer blend and GQD were characterized using FTIR. Fig. 4 presents the FTIR spectra of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS} polymer blend and various wt.% loading of GQD (0.5-1.5 wt.%) in the ternary polymer composite system. The typical spectrum of polymer blend {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} (Fig. 4a) showed some of the IR signals of various functional groups like v(-OH) and v(-NH) stretching at 3400 cm−1, CH=CH stretching at 2910-2862 cm−1, C=C stretching at 1410 cm−1 of thiophene ring, S=O stretching at 1367 cm−1, C-O stretching at 1230-1097 cm−1, =C-H & =CH2 stretching at 995 cm−1, C-H bending at 796 cm−1, C-S stretching at 660 cm−1 in agreement with reports [35]. Fig. 4(b-d) represents the FTIR spectrum of various wt.% GQD loaded (from 0.5-1.5 wt.%) polymer system. The composites exhibited qualitatively similar IR peak assignments with slight variation (±5 cm−1) in the frequency region. A major change was observed for the higher loading of {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1.5 wt.%} composite when compared with {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS} blend (Fig. 4a) in the frequency region at 3387 cm−1 and 1103 cm−1 corresponding to the vOH and vNH and C-O stretching vibrations. This observation is due to the strong hydrogen bondings interaction between the oxygen-rich functional group present in GQD (-COOH, -OH) and other heteroatoms (N-H, S=O and C-Η) of the ternary polymer matrix.

Raman spectra of pure PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS polymer blend and GQD (0.5 to 1.5 wt%) loaded PVB+PANI+PEDOT: PSS composite systems are shown in Fig. 5 (a-d). The Raman spectrum of pure polymer blend (Fig 5a) exhibits two prominent peaks, D (sp3) and G (sp2) bands at 1335.39 and 1572.37 cm−1 corresponding to the disorder and ordered graphitic like structural units of PANI and PEDOT:PSS combined units [36,37]. As the loading concentration of GQD in the polymer matrix increases from 0.5-1.5 wt% (Fig 5 b-d), the D band peak intensity increases markedly. This observation is corresponding to the combinatory effect of graphitic (πe-s conjugation) and non-graphic (oxygen functional site) fine structures of Polymer-GQD composite system [38,39]. In addition to the regular D and G bands, there are some overtones/additional peaks were also observed in Fig. 5, which may be due to the heterogeneity like the molecular structure of the polymer-carbon nanocomposite system [40]. The optimal composite {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} may exhibit compromised levels and D and G bands which is highly desirable for electrical applications.

In order to get the surface feature of the optimal polymer-GQD composite film surface feature, a laser-optical profilometric experiment was carried out in comparison with GQD free polymer matrix system. For that, a piece of 1 cm2 area polymeric material was subjected to the surface profiling experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 A, before GQD loading, the polymer matrix system showed a low-surface profile 3D image, whereas, after the loading (Fig. 6B), a high surface contour like the image was noticed. Increased heterogeneity like the structural arrangement of a polymer-GQD composite is the possible reason for the observation.

The electrochemical property of the polymer blend was tested by a cyclic voltammetric technique using ferricyanide as a redox probe in 0.5 M KCl solution. It is noteworthy that ferricyanide/ferrocyanide redox couple is a benchmarking system in electrochemistry and by studying its redox feature on solid electrodes one can get information about the electrical and electrochemical properties of the solid test material [41]. Here, the polymer blend modified screen-printed electrode (SPCE) was taken as a test system. In general, at an ideal solid surface, the ferricyanide compound shows a well-defined redox peak (Faradic reaction) at a standard electrode potential, Eo = 0.25±0.005 V vs Ag/AgCl with forward (anodic i pa) and reverse (cathodic, i pc) peak current magnitude of unity (i pa/i pc ~1) and anodic & cathodic peak-potential separation (ΔEp =Epa-Epc, wherein, Epa and Epc are anodic and cathodic peak potential values) of 60 mV (for one-electron-transfer reaction) [41]. By studying the above redox reaction on the test-solid electrode, one can get to know about the electrochemical properties of the sample. Fig. 7 , a-c are CV responses of PANI PEDOT:PSS, GQD, PVB/PANI/PEDOT.PSS and different loading of GQD in polymer blend samples, that have been modified on SPCE, with 5 mM of ferricyanide complex in 0.5 M KCl solution. Only PEDOT:PSS and GQD modified electrodes showed well-defined redox peaks under the test conditions indicating the good electrical property of the material, as reported in the previous literature reports [42,43]. Other composite materials modified SPCE showed poor and nil current responses with the ferricyanide systems. The possible reason for the observation is due to the high capacitive current response (~1-10 mA cm-2; Non-faradaic response) of the blended electrodes. The faradic redox (2 mA cm−2) behavior is found to be buried in the substantial capacitive current response. This result indicates the opportunity for the extension of this new material for supercapacitor application.

Fig. 8 a and b are typical comparative electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) responses of a polymer blend with several control samples. This experiment was carried out with SPCE modified polymer blend samples along with 5 mM ferricyanide redox couple in 0.5 M KCl solution at a biased potential, 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl, wherein, the ferricyanide complex is converted to ferrocyanide complex via one-electron-transfer reaction, i.e., FeIII(CN)6 3−+e−↔ FeII(CN)6 4−. In all those experiments, a feeble semicircle followed by a linear increase in the line like a response, which is due to the resistance of charge-transfer electron reaction (RCT, Faradic reaction; electron-transfer reaction) and equivalent series resistance (Eqsr; Non-Faradic) systems respectively, was noticed. The Eqsr can be correlated to a series of resistances of the electrolyte solution, intrinsic resistance of the electrode material, and resistance at the interface between the electrode and current collector [25]. Using an inbuilt software program, the EIS responses were curve-fitted using Randles electrical circuit model, Rs[Cdl(RCTW1)], wherein, Rs is the resistance corresponding to the Eqsr and Cdl, and W1 are double-layer capacitance and Warburg diffusion impedance for the ferricyanide electron-transfer reaction [44]. Table 1 is a collective-data for the Eqsr and RCT values obtained for pure individual systems, polymer blend and control samples. A marked decrement in the Eqsr (88.65 Ω) and RCT (2.52 Ω) values were noticed with the polymer blend that has been loaded with 1 wt.% of GQD. It is proposed that the 1 wt.% of GQD modified polymer blend {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} is the best system with optimal loading of the individual component. As discussed in the FTIR analysis results, there will be a maximum hydrogen bonding effect with the optimal system and hence showed superior electrochemical behavior than that of the other control and GQD loaded (0.5 and 1.5 wt.%) composites. The attractive physicochemical property of the 1wt.% GQD loaded polymer blend sample was explored for supercapacitor applications.

At first, CV analyses of the polymer blend and control samples were performed in 0.1 M H2SO4. In general, the capacitor-like material modified electrode shows a huge non-Faradic current signal with a box-like current feature (without any peak current response), whereas the Faradaic system shows a large electron-transfer reaction with a specific peak current signal [45]. Fig. 9 a-c is the typical CV response of the polymer blend, GQD composite systems and other control samples. The GQD modified polymer composite samples (Fig. 9c) showed a characteristic behavior with a huge current response, unlike the other control samples (Fig. 9a-b) highlighting relatively less current response involving with Faradic behavior. Amongst the various loading, 1 wt.% loaded GQD + polymer blend sample {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} resulted in the maximum non-faradic current response. This observation is in parallel with the electrochemical characterization results noticed with the CV and EIS results. Meanwhile, specific redox signals noticed with the PEDOT:PSS and PANI (Figs. 9a and b) are correlated to the radical species [46] and proton-transfer-electron-transfer responses [47,48] respectively. Indeed, after the modification, the electron-transfer features are buried inside the huge non-faradic process of the composite material. The maximum chemical interaction noticed with the GQD and polymer blend is the key reason for the optimal electrochemical response. To quantify the supercapacitor characteristic, the composite modified electrodes were subjected to galvanostatic charge-discharge analysis, as discussed in the below section.

Fig. 10 a (i-iv) exhibit charge/discharge curves of pure systems of PANI, PEDOT:PSS, GQD and {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} blend those were all modified on SPCE surfaces and tested in 0.1 M H2SO4 at a biased current density of 0.14 Ag−1. As can be seen that all the samples exhibit a triangle shape but to a different extent. Compared with individual samples, the GQD loaded composites (GQD-0.5-1.5wt.%) displayed in Fig. 10b (i-iii) exhibit about five-time improved charge-discharge responses. Furthermore, the {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} blend loaded with 1 wt.% GQD {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} exhibited the largest charge storage time (1300±10 sec) when compared with pure PANI, GQD, PEDOT:PSS, and blend systems. Hence {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} composite is chosen to be the optimal sample. Furthermore, to confirm the individual capacitance value of the prepared samples, the specific capacitance (C s) was calculated using the standard formula [49].
(1)
C s
=
I ×
Δ
t
m ×
Δ
V
Wherein, Cs - specific capacitance (F g−1), I - applied current (A), ∆t - discharge time (s), ∆V- change in potential (V), and m - the mass of active material coated on the electrode (g). Table 2 provides the quantitative data for the specific capacitor (C s) values. Following are the summary of the C s values; 1427 Fg−1cm−2 (100.90 Fg−1) for {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS} blend, 628.63 Fg−1cm−2 (44 F g−1) for {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 0.5 wt.%}, 4992.1 Fg−1cm−2 (352.94 Fg−1) for {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%}, 2183.74 Fg−1cm−2 (154.39 Fg−1) for {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1.5 wt.%} respectively. It is obvious to see the highest performance with the 1wt.% GQD modified polymer blend system. The value obtained in this work for the optimal composite system is relatively higher than the values reported with other polymer blend/graphene composite materials as shown in Table 3 ; 291.28 Fg−1 with PANI/PVA hydrogel (at 1 Ag−1) [50], 262.5 Fg−1 with PANI/PPy/rGO (at 1 Ag−1) [51], 356 Fg−1 PANI-PHQ/RGO (at 0.5 Ag−1) [52], 801.1 Fg−1 with PANI/MWCNTs/PVC (at 1 Ag−1) [53], 32 Fg−1 PVA/PEDOT:PSS (at 0.5 Ag−1) [54], 291.9 Fg−1 PVA/GQD/PEDOT (at 2 Ag−1) [25], 105 Fg−1 PAN/PMMA/CNF (at 2 Ag−1) [55], 160 Fg−1 PVP/PVB blend (at 1 Ag−1) [56],174.7 Fg−1 PF-Zn-PVB (at 0.5 Ag−1) [57] respectively. There is ~0.2 V of potential drop noticed in the intersection point of the charge-discharge curves of the PVB modified composite electrodes. The existence of grain-boundary and its partial resistance is the likely reason for the observation. It is a limitation of the PVB to be used in the supercapacitor application. A detailed study is necessary to explore and to rectify the IR drop [58,59]. Regarding the synergism between the polymers, PANI's contribution towards the pseudocapacitance is significant, whereas, the PEDOT:PSS showed very poor capacitance response (Fig. 10a). Hence, there is no synergism with respect the PEDOT:PSS. On the other hand, the role of PEDOT:PSS is a matrix to stabilize the multicomponent polymer on the electrode surface and for improved electrical conductivity value.

In order to understand the electron-transfer behavior and pseudocapacitance, the effect of scan rate experiment with PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.% sample as in Fig. 11 a. A regular increment in pseudo-capacitance behavior was noticed at v= 2-10 mV s−1, a large deviation in the current behavior was noticed, which may be due to some kinetic limitation within the film. In addition, the effect of current density on the pseudo-capacitance was analyzed by varying the applied current bias as 100 and 200 μA on the modified electrode (Fig. 11b). A reverse in trend, wherein, high bias current showed less in pseudo-capacitance and vice-versa with a low bias current was noticed.

Finally, workability and long term stability of the optimal system {PVB/PANI/PEDOT: PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} was tested by subjecting the electrode material for continuous charge-discharge cycles up to 9000 sec at an applied bias current 100 and 200 μA in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. 12
a & b). A stable and unaltered potential vs time response was noticed, highlighting the retainment of the charge-capacity and suitability of this new material for practical supercapacitor applications.

We have prepared a novel ternary polymer nanocomposite by incorporating an optimal loading concentration of PVB and GQD into PANI/PEDOT:PSS polymers mixed matrix. As a result, a notable influence on the absorbance trend was observed for all the composites from UV-Vis spectroscopy. The absorbance intensity of the optimal composite, {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} is found to be lesser than that of 0.5 and 1.5 wt.% loadings composites illustrating the possible interaction between the GQD/polymer system and its tunable optical property for the specific loading effect. In the XRD analysis, both the polymer blends without and with GQD loading exhibited a broad peak 2θ=42±2ο with no marked changes in the diffraction pattern supporting the heterogeneous nature of the composite. GQD's distribution in the polymer matrix and their average particle size has been detected as 6±2nm from TEM morphological images. This value is found to be about five-times lesser than that of the pristine GQD (50±5nm), supporting the chemical interaction between the polymeric system and GQD. In fact, the functional groups interaction and intermolecular hydrogen bonding between polymer matrix and GQD lead to remarkable changes in FTIR signals. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopic characterization with ferricyanide based benchmark redox couple results of equivalent series resistance and RCT value reduced markedly when compared with the pristine systems. Because of these enhanced physicochemical properties, {PVB/PANI/PEDOT:PSS+GQD 1 wt.%} composite showed an improved capacitance (pseudo) value, 4992.1 Fg−1 cm−2 at 0.14 Ag−1 over the pure polymer and other PANI based literature reports. The electrochemical stability of this optimal composite was tested by performing continuous charge-discharge cycling up to 3 hrs showing unaltered potential-time response. Overall, this new PVB based ternary polymer-GQD composite displays an improved pseudo-supercapacitance performance and functional stability suitable for a variety of tunable electronic applications.

D. Arthisree: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. N. Saravanan: Support for the GQD synthesis; Resources, Data curation, Visualization. B. Dinesh: Support for the capacitor related studies. S.Saikrithika: Support for the optical profilometer imaging of the polymer-composite, Resources, Data curation, Visualization. W. Madhuri: Methodology, Validation, Resources, Visualization, Review & Editing. Annamalai Senthil Kumar: Methodology, Writing – final draft, Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: ASK acknowledges the Department of Science and Technology – Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB-EMR/2016/002818) Scheme for the financial support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyvinylpyrrolidone/polyvinyl butyral composite as a stable binder for castable supercapacitor electrodes in aqueous electrolytes
